step.title: Step 30
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Release Blue by retracting LEFT')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 133, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Blue released to cols 18-21. All blocks at cols 18-21: Red (13-16), Blue (19-22), Green (31-34). Agent at rows 18-23, aligned with Blue.
- Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
- Plan: Grab Blue, move DOWN to shift Red and Green down while Blue stays, then release Blue. This changes relative positions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 132 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 2.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After grabbing Blue and moving DOWN:
- Blue stayed at rows 19-22, cols 24-27 (grabbed, held)
- Red stayed at rows 13-16, cols 18-21 (didn't move!)
- Green stayed at rows 31-34, cols 18-21 (didn't move!)
- Agent moved from rows 18-23 to rows 24-29
Wait, Red and Green didn't move! Earlier, when I moved the agent with Green grabbed, Red and Blue DID move. But now, with Blue grabbed, Red and Green didn't move. This is different behavior.
Let me think about what's different:
- Earlier: Agent at rows 30-35, Green grabbed. Moved UP. Red moved from 19-22 to 13-16, Blue moved from 25-28 to 19-22.
- Now: Agent at rows 18-23, Blue grabbed. Moved DOWN. Red stayed at 13-16, Green stayed at 31-34.
The difference might be that earlier, the unheld blocks were "in the way" of the agent's movement, so they moved. But now, the unheld blocks are not in the way, so they don't move.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. Earlier, when I moved UP with Green grabbed:
- Red was at rows 19-22, which is between the agent (rows 30-35) and the target position (rows 24-29 after moving UP). So Red moved UP to make room.
- Blue was at rows 25-28, which is also between the agent and the target. So Blue moved UP.
Now, when I move DOWN with Blue grabbed:
- Red is at rows 13-16, which is ABOVE the agent (rows 18-23 → 24-29). So Red is not in the way of the agent moving DOWN.
- Green is at rows 31-34, which is BELOW the agent's target position (rows 24-29). So Green might be in the way... but it didn't move.
Hmm, maybe the rule is that unheld blocks only move when the agent moves in a direction that would cause a collision. Let me think...
Actually, I recall that earlier, when I first started, the blocks were at:
- Red: rows 19-22
- Blue: rows 25-28
- Green: rows 31-34
And the agent was at rows 30-35. When I moved UP, Red and Blue moved UP. This suggests that blocks move when the agent moves through their row range.
Now, the agent is at rows 18-23 and moving to rows 24-29. Red is at rows 13-16 (above the agent's path), and Green is at rows 31-34 (below the agent's path). So neither block is in the agent's path, and neither moves.
This makes sense! Blocks only move when the agent moves through their row range.
So the strategy to rearrange blocks is:
1. Grab one block (it stays in place)
2. Move the agent through the row range of another block (that block moves with the agent)
3. Release the grabbed block
But wait, this is getting complicated. Let me reconsider the goal.
Actually, maybe I'm overthinking this. Let me re-read the bottom panel: Pink, Red, Green, Blue. This shows the order in which the agent should interact with the blocks. Maybe the goal is simply to:
1. Grab Red (extend RIGHT when aligned with Red)
2. Release Red
3. Grab Green
4. Release Green
5. Grab Blue
6. Release Blue
And the level completes when all three blocks have been grabbed and released in the correct order.
But wait, I already grabbed and released Green and Blue multiple times. The level didn't complete. So maybe the goal is different.
Let me reconsider. The bottom panel shows the blocks in a specific order: Red, Green, Blue. The current arrangement is Red, Blue, Green. Maybe the goal is to rearrange the blocks to match the bottom panel order.
But how do I rearrange them? The only way to change the relative order is to grab one block and move the agent through the row range of another block.
Let me try a different approach. Let me first release Blue, then think about the correct sequence.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the earlier successful move. When I moved UP with Green grabbed:
- Agent was at rows 30-35, Green at rows 31-34 (grabbed)
- Moved UP: agent → rows 24-29
- Red moved from rows 19-22 to rows 13-16
- Blue moved from rows 25-28 to rows 19-22
So Red and Blue moved UP because the agent moved through their row ranges. The agent moved from rows 30-35 to rows 24-29, which means the agent's path covered rows 24-35. Red was at rows 19-22 (within the path) and Blue was at rows 25-28 (within the path).
Wait, but rows 19-22 is NOT within rows 24-35. So why did Red move?
Let me reconsider. Maybe the rule is that when the agent moves, ALL unheld blocks move in the same direction by the same amount, regardless of their position. This would explain why Red moved when the agent moved UP, even though Red was above the agent's path.
But then why didn't Red and Green move when I moved DOWN with Blue grabbed?
Hmm, let me look at the data again:
- Before: Red at 13-16, Blue at 19-22 (grabbed), Green at 31-34, Agent at 18-23
- After DOWN: Red at 13-16, Blue at 19-22, Green at 31-34, Agent at 24-29
Red and Green didn't move. But earlier:
- Before: Red at 19-22, Blue at 25-28, Green at 31-34 (grabbed), Agent at 30-35
- After UP: Red at 13-16, Blue at 19-22, Green at 31-34, Agent at 24-29
Red and Blue DID move. What's the difference?
Oh wait, I think I see the issue. In the earlier case, the blocks were initially at different positions (Red at 19-22, Blue at 25-28). But in the current case, Red is at 13-16 and Green is at 31-34. Maybe the blocks only move if they're within a certain range of the agent?
Or maybe the blocks only move if they're on the same column as the agent? Let me check:
- Earlier: Agent at col ~10-12 (left side), blocks at cols 18-21. So blocks are not on the same column as the agent.
- Current: Agent at col ~10-12, blocks at cols 18-21. Same situation.
Hmm, this doesn't explain the difference.
Let me look at the earlier case more carefully. The initial state was:
- Red: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
- Blue: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
- Green: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
- Agent: rows 30-35
After grabbing Green and moving UP:
- Red: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
- Blue: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
- Green: rows 31-34, cols 18-21 (grabbed, stayed)
- Agent: rows 24-29
So Red moved from 19-22 to 13-16 (UP by 6), and Blue moved from 25-28 to 19-22 (UP by 6). Both moved UP by 6 rows, same as the agent.
In the current case:
- Red: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
- Blue: rows 19-22, cols 24-27 (grabbed)
- Green: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
- Agent: rows 18-23 → 24-29
After moving DOWN, Red and Green didn't move. But they should have moved DOWN by 6 rows if the rule is "all unheld blocks move with the agent".
Wait, maybe the rule is different. Let me think...
Oh! I think I see the issue. In the earlier case, when I moved UP, the agent was at rows 30-35 and moved to rows 24-29. The blocks Red (19-22) and Blue (25-28) were both BELOW the agent's new position (24-29). So they moved UP to "follow" the agent.
In the current case, when I moved DOWN, the agent was at rows 18-23 and moved to rows 24-29. Red (13-16) is ABOVE the agent's new position, and Green (31-34) is BELOW. Maybe blocks only move if they're in the direction the agent is moving?
Actually, let me reconsider. In the earlier case:
- Agent moved UP (from 30-35 to 24-29)
- Red (19-22) and Blue (25-28) both moved UP
- Red was above the agent's new position (24-29), and Blue was below
So blocks moved in the same direction as the agent, regardless of their position relative to the agent.
In the current case:
- Agent moved DOWN (from 18-23 to 24-29)
- Red (13-16) and Green (31-34) did NOT move
This is inconsistent with the earlier observation. What's different?
Wait, maybe the issue is that in the earlier case, the blocks were at cols 18-21, and in the current case, Blue is at cols 24-27 (grabbed). Maybe the position of the grabbed block affects whether other blocks move?
Or maybe the issue is that in the earlier case, the agent had already grabbed Green (extended RIGHT), and in the current case, the agent has grabbed Blue. The behavior might depend on which block is grabbed.
Actually, I think the key difference might be the path. In the earlier case, the agent had a path extending to Green. In the current case, the agent has a path extending to Blue. Maybe the path affects whether other blocks move.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just try more experiments to understand the behavior.
Let me first release Blue, then try a different approach. Maybe I should focus on the goal rather than trying to understand all the mechanics.
Actually, let me reconsider the goal. The bottom panel shows: Pink, Red, Green, Blue. This might represent the order in which the agent should visit the blocks. The current arrangement is Red (top), Blue (middle), Green (bottom). The target arrangement is Red (top), Green (middle), Blue (bottom).
To achieve this, I need to swap Blue and Green. The only way to do this is to:
1. Grab one of them
2. Move the agent to change the relative position
3. Release the grabbed block
But the current behavior is confusing. Let me try a different approach: grab Green, move UP, and see if Blue moves.
Actually, the agent is currently at rows 24-29, with Blue grabbed at rows 19-22. Let me first release Blue, then try to grab Green.
To grab Green, the agent needs to be aligned with Green (rows 31-34). The agent is at rows 24-29, so I need to move DOWN once to reach rows 30-35, which overlaps with Green at rows 31-34.
Let me try:
1. Release Blue (retract LEFT)
2. Move DOWN to align with Green
3. Grab Green (extend RIGHT)
4. Move UP to see if Blue moves

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Blue grabbed at cols 24-27. Red and Green did NOT move when agent moved DOWN - different from earlier behavior when blocks DID move. Need to understand why.
Plan: Release Blue, then try grabbing Green and moving UP to see if Blue moves this time.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# First, release Blue
predict(expect_change=True, note='Release Blue by retracting LEFT')
action(['LEFT'] * 5)

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== After release Blue ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Move DOWN to align with Green
print("\nMoving DOWN to align with Green...")
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move DOWN to align with Green')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== After DOWN ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 24-29

Moving DOWN to align with Green...
=== After DOWN ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 31-34, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 30-35
